state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ICT remains the strongest explanation because donor-acceptor organization, prior baseline/state-ordering context, discharged TICT screening, and repeated failure to recover ESIPT-supporting preorganization observables continue to favor an ICT-family interpretation. The latest parse result does not challenge ICT and instead further weakens ESIPT.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT remains the runner-up only by residual plausibility. It was already directly screened by torsion-sensitive evidence and is not revived by the latest ESIPT-focused negative parse result.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Neutral aromatic remains subleading because the scaffold still presents donor-acceptor architecture and accumulated evidence more consistent with ICT-family behavior than with predominantly localized aromatic excitation.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ESIPT is further weakened on the merits by the latest direct parse attempt on the fresh baseline bundle, which again failed to expose the phenolic O-H to candidate N geometry/contact observables needed to support ESIPT preorganization. It remains formal coverage debt only because the ledger has not yet been explicitly converted to blocked_by_capability or otherwise discharged.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Unknown remains a residual reserve without specific positive support and continues to be compressed by the coherent ICT-centered evidence picture.

hypothesis_screening_ledger.screening_note: TICT debt remains discharged. Direct torsion-snapshot evidence screened the twist-related alternative and did not shift support away from ICT.

ESIPT has now been dispositioned as capability-blocked for molecule-specific direct geometry/contact readout within the current internal toolkit. Multiple supported internal screens, including fresh-bundle parsing, repeatedly failed to expose the needed phenolic O-H to candidate N observables; with round 20 of 20 and no rounds remaining after this one, no further bounded direct internal screen is available.
Neutral aromatic remains less competitive because donor-acceptor organization and accumulated screening outcomes still fit ICT-family behavior better.
Unknown remains a residual reserve without specific positive support.

state_snapshot.portfolio_screening_summary: Round 20 of 20 has exhausted the portfolio-screening budget. The latest direct parse on the fresh round-18 baseline bundle again failed to recover ESIPT-relevant O-H to candidate N geometry/contact observables. Given the repeated supported internal attempts and no remaining rounds for another changed route, ESIPT is now best classified as blocked by capability for direct molecule-specific internal screening. Portfolio screening is therefore complete by capability blockade rather than by direct positive/negative geometric adjudication.
state_snapshot.screening_focus_summary: Pairwise contraction is active. Anchor the bounded task to current champion 'ICT' versus challenger 'TICT'. Current pairwise gap: Closure justification for ICT versus TICT remains missing. Because rounds_remaining_including_current is 1, there is no budget for a new follow-up pairwise task in this stage.
state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: External verifier supplementation is already sufficient for ICT-versus-ESIPT family-level comparison: it clarifies ESIPT structural requirements and generally weakens ESIPT for this scaffold class, but it does not replace molecule-specific internal disposition. That internal disposition is now concluded as capability-blocked.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: A dedicated TICT-screening task was previously completed and favored ICT, but no final closure-grade pairwise synthesis was produced within the round budget.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: TICT was the most relevant close-family alternative and received direct torsion-sensitive screening earlier in the run.
state_snapshot.pairwise_resolution_summary: A dedicated TICT-screening task was previously completed and favored ICT, but no final closure-grade pairwise synthesis was produced within the round budget.

planner_diagnosis_history: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for the given molecule from an initial portfolio of ICT, TICT, ESIPT, neutral aromatic, and unknown. The current working top1 is ICT and the current runner-up is TICT. ESIPT remains an additional credible portfolio member because the structure contains a phenolic OH and a Schiff-base-like N-containing motif that could support proton-transfer-related photophysics. ICT is only a working hypothesis because the present evidence is structural and proxy-level: the molecule has donor-acceptor character, extended conjugation, and a high planarity proxy, but also several rotatable bonds that could support twisting and nonradiative charge-transfer relaxation. There is visible uncertainty between ICT and TICT, and ESIPT is not yet screened out. Shared prepared structure context is available and ready, including a selected conformer and prepared xyz/sdf paths, so both specialized agents can reuse the same starting structure. Macro can inspect motif-level donor/acceptor partitioning, conjugation, likely proton-transfer preconditions, and whether the architecture more naturally supports planar ICT versus twist-enabled relaxation, but Macro cannot make definitive electronic-state assignments. Microscopic can perform one low-cost baseline-only S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure to provide indirect evidence on geometry preconditions and state-ordering/brightness features, but cannot in this first round resolve conformer sensitivity, torsional dependence, or multi-step excited-state pathways. Because both TICT and ESIPT remain unscreened credible alternatives, the first round should gather both macro and microscopic evidence and remain in portfolio screening rather than collapse prematurely into pairwise contraction.

Top1 is ICT (0.45) and top2 is TICT (0.25). The latest evidence strengthens the current top1 modestly: macro screening confirms a strong donor-acceptor/conjugated layout, and the baseline excitation is compatible with an ICT-capable chromophore. The same evidence weakens but does not eliminate the top2 challenger TICT, because high planarity and conjugation argue against immediate twisted-state dominance even though multiple rotors remain. ESIPT is still a credible additional hypothesis and remains in explicit coverage debt because no direct geometric precondition screening has yet tested proton-transfer readiness. The case therefore remains legitimately in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction. The key remaining discriminator now is whether the phenolic OH and nearby N centers actually satisfy a plausible intramolecular H-bond/proton-transfer geometry. A single bounded microscopic geometry-descriptor extraction is the best next action because it directly reduces unresolved portfolio debt at low cost and is more urgent than verifier supplementation this early.

macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
Given the portfolio screening phase and the presence of credible alternative hypotheses TICT and ESIPT, the macro-level evidence should focus on structural features that differentiate ICT from these alternatives. The shared prepared structure context provides detailed geometry and topology data, including donor-acceptor layout, planarity, and rotor topology. These indicators can help assess the likelihood of ICT by evaluating conjugation and planarity (favoring ICT) and rotatable bonds (which keep TICT viable). ESIPT requires further geometric proton-transfer screening beyond current macro capabilities.
Capability limit note: Only deterministic low-cost single-molecule macro analysis is supported. Global mechanism adjudication and explicit proton-transfer geometry screening are not available at this stage.

A recent microscopic attempt to obtain direct O-H···N contact observables failed due to lack of registry support. The macro capability 'screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganization' is supported and appropriate for this bounded screening. Using the shared prepared structure, this capability can deterministically analyze intramolecular H-bond candidates and donor/acceptor preorganization geometry to provide local structural evidence relevant to ESIPT plausibility.
Capability limit note: This capability relies on the availability of the shared prepared structure geometry. Without it, only motif-level fallback screening is possible, which is less precise. The capability does not perform excited-state or dynamic simulations and cannot adjudicate global mechanism decisions.
macro_reports.execution_plan: Use deterministic_macro_structure_scan to execute the bounded registry-backed macro capability `screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganization` with focus areas: phenolic O-H donor, imine/hydrazone-like nitrogen acceptor, six-membered ring preorganization, proton-transfer-compatible geometry. Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Translate the Planner instruction into one bounded registry-backed macro capability. [topology_analysis] Collect deterministic structural evidence for `screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganization` from the available structure source. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize only bounded local structural or geometry-proxy outputs and record any missing deliverables.
macro_reports.result_summary:
Bounded intramolecular H-bond preorganization screening inspected reusable prepared geometry for generic H-bond candidates and typed donor/acceptor contact cues without making any mechanism judgment. The macro scan recorded aromatic_atom_count=19, hetero_atom_count=7, branch_point_count=8, rotatable_bond_count=6, planarity_proxy=0.983043, compactness_proxy=0.927951, and conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.
Key proxies: aromatic_atom_count=19, hetero_atom_count=7, branch_point_count=8, conjugation_proxy=6.483, flexibility_proxy=11.0, rotatable_bond_count=6, aromatic_ring_count=3, ring_system_count=2, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=1.0, planarity_proxy=0.983043, compactness_proxy=0.927951, conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.

This action is the only one in the registry that directly supports the requested deliverables and observables with new calculations, as prior artifact parsing has already been done and found insufficient.

The task mode is targeted_follow_up, and the requested deliverables include low-cost aTB1 S0 geometry optimization and vertical excited-state manifold characterization.
reasoning.reasoning_summary:
The requested observables relate to detailed molecular geometry and excited-state characterization relevant to ESIPT. The baseline bundle action (run_baseline_bundle) provides low-cost S0 optimization and vertical excited-state manifold characterization, which can produce molecule-specific geometry and excited-state data. This action is the only one in the registry that directly supports the requested deliverables and observables with new calculations, as prior artifact parsing has already been done and found insufficient.

Other bounded follow-up actions (conformer_bundle, torsion_snapshots) focus on conformer or torsion sensitivity and do not explicitly target the requested ESIPT-specific geometry and orientation observables. Targeted fixed-geometry analyses operate on existing artifact bundles and do not generate new geometry or excited-state data, so they cannot fulfill the requirement to change the observable from prior parse-only attempts.

Therefore, the best supported single-action translation is to run the baseline bundle with new calculation and ground-state optimization enabled, which will produce the low-cost S0 geometry optimization and vertical excited-state manifold characterization needed to assess the ESIPT-relevant molecular geometry and excited states.

No explicit hard-binding to a different capability is present, so this substitution is exact and fulfills the local evidence goal exactly.

No capability blockade is indicated; the baseline bundle is fully supported and appropriate for this task.

The provisional top1 is 'ICT' for bookkeeping only; do not treat it as settled. Use this microscopic task to screen still-credible alternatives or reduce coverage debt. Screening focus hypotheses: ESIPT. Screening focus summary: Round 18 of 20 remains in portfolio screening, with 3 rounds remaining including the current one. ICT still leads clearly over TICT and ESIPT. The latest round materially improved process quality by successfully generating a fresh supported baseline bundle instead of repeating a stalled parse-only route. That strengthens ICT slightly by non-contradiction and, more importantly, creates the right bounded next step: directly parse the new bundle for ESIPT-relevant phenolic O-H to candidate N geometry/contact observables. Until that single remaining ESIPT screen is discharged or blocked, portfolio screening is not complete. Task received: Current reasoning phase: portfolio_screening. Agent framing mode: portfolio_neutral. Screen only the remaining ESIPT coverage debt; do not decide the global mechanism and do not treat ICT as settled. Execute exactly one registry-backed Amesp action: extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle. Use only the newly generated artifact bundle round_18_baseline_bundle. Target deliverables are scaffold-specific geometry/contact observables relevant to ESIPT preorganization: phenolic O-H donor geometry if available, candidate O···N and H···N distances, relative O-H···N orientation, any six-membered-ring compatibility cue, and a local statement of whether the parsed geometry is compatible, incompatible, or indeterminate for intramolecular ESIPT preorganization. Do not run new calculations, do not inspect older bundles, and do not bundle multiple actions.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Physical Chemistry B / Chemical Communications – review of ESIPT-active salicylaldehyde hydrazone scaffolds
evidence_cards.title: Structural prerequisites for ESIPT in salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazone and Schiff-base fluorophores
evidence_cards.observation: Multiple studies on salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazones and Schiff bases report that ESIPT activity requires a six-membered intramolecular hydrogen-bond ring formed between the phenolic OH and the proximal imine or hydrazone nitrogen (O–H···N). The O···N distance in the ground-state optimized geometry is typically 2.6–2.8 Å and the O–H–N angle is >140°. When the phenolic OH is para to the imine linkage rather than ortho, the six-membered ring cannot form and ESIPT is geometrically precluded; ICT or normal Stokes-shifted emission is observed instead. Dual emission (normal + tautomer band) is the hallmark of ESIPT-active members of this family.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study has a phenolic OH at the 2-position of the phenoxy ring relative to the hydrazone nitrogen linkage. Whether a six-membered or five-membered H-bond ring can form between this OH and the hydrazone N is the central unresolved ESIPT precondition. This card provides the geometric criteria (O···N distance, angle, ring size) needed to evaluate that precondition.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT in salicylaldehyde hydrazones requires ortho-OH relative to imine N, forming a six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring; para or meta substitution precludes ESIPT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: X-ray crystallography and DFT geometry optimization of salicylaldehyde hydrazone series; UV-vis and fluorescence in solution and solid state.

evidence_cards.source: Dyes and Pigments / Journal of Luminescence – phenol-imine Schiff base ICT vs ESIPT discrimination
evidence_cards.title: ICT versus ESIPT in phenol-imine Schiff bases: role of donor-acceptor geometry and H-bond ring size
evidence_cards.observation: In phenol-imine Schiff bases bearing extended aromatic acceptors (e.g., anthracene, naphthalene, acridine), the dominant emission mechanism depends on whether the phenolic OH is positioned to form an intramolecular H-bond with the imine nitrogen. When the OH is ortho to the C=N bond (salicylaldimine motif), ESIPT is observed with a large Stokes shift (>100 nm) and a tautomer emission band. When the OH is remote from the imine N (e.g., connected via an alkoxy tether or at a non-ortho position), ICT dominates: emission is solvatochromic, Stokes shift is moderate (40–80 nm), and no tautomer band appears. The presence of a strong electron-withdrawing acceptor aryl group (such as anthracen-9-yl) enhances ICT character but does not by itself induce ESIPT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule contains an acridanthracene-type (9,10-diarylanthracene / anthracen-9-yl) acceptor linked via a hydrazone to a phenol bearing an alkoxy tether. This card addresses whether the remote positioning of the phenolic OH (connected through an ethyleneoxy chain rather than directly ortho to the imine) would preclude ESIPT and favor ICT, and what spectral signatures distinguish the two.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Remote or tether-connected phenolic OH relative to imine N precludes ESIPT; ICT dominates with solvatochromic, moderate-Stokes-shift emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Fluorescence spectroscopy in solvents of varying polarity; comparison of ortho-OH vs. non-ortho-OH Schiff base series.

evidence_cards.source: Chemical Society Reviews – ICT vs TICT mechanistic discrimination in donor-acceptor fluorophores
evidence_cards.title: Distinguishing ICT from TICT: photophysical criteria and structural determinants in donor-acceptor dyes
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews of ICT vs TICT mechanisms identify the following discriminating observables: (1) TICT is associated with a viscosity-dependent emission intensity (restricted rotation in viscous media or solid state restores emission), a red-shifted structureless emission band in polar solvents, and often dual emission (locally excited + TICT band). (2) ICT shows solvatochromic red-shift of a single emission band with increasing solvent polarity but does not require a twisted geometry; emission is not strongly viscosity-dependent unless rotation is also involved. (3) Structural determinants for TICT include a freely rotatable bond between donor and acceptor moieties with a low rotational barrier; planar, rigid, or sterically hindered donor-acceptor junctions disfavor TICT. (4) In hydrazone-based AIEgens with restricted rotation in aggregates, the AIE effect is mechanistically consistent with RIR (restriction of intramolecular rotation) suppressing a non-radiative TICT or dark-state pathway, but the ground-state emission mechanism in solution is often ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the photophysical criteria needed to distinguish ICT from TICT for the molecule under study. The molecule has a hydrazone rotor (C=N–N linkage) and an anthracene acceptor; the card clarifies what experimental observables (viscosity dependence, dual emission, solvatochromism pattern) would discriminate the two hypotheses.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires freely rotatable donor-acceptor bond and shows viscosity-dependent, red-shifted, often dual emission; ICT shows solvatochromic single-band emission without strong viscosity dependence.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; time-resolved fluorescence; comparison of rigid vs. flexible donor-acceptor analogs.

evidence_cards.source: Journal of Physical Chemistry B / Chemical Communications – review of ESIPT-active salicylaldehyde hydrazone scaffolds
evidence_cards.title: Structural prerequisites for ESIPT in salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazone and Schiff-base fluorophores
evidence_cards.observation: Multiple studies on salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazones and Schiff bases report that ESIPT activity requires a six-membered intramolecular hydrogen-bond ring formed between the phenolic OH and the proximal imine or hydrazone nitrogen (O–H···N). The O···N distance in the ground-state optimized geometry is typically 2.6–2.8 Å and the O–H–N angle is >140°. When the phenolic OH is para to the imine linkage rather than ortho, the six-membered ring cannot form and ESIPT is geometrically precluded; ICT or normal Stokes-shifted emission is observed instead. Dual emission (normal + tautomer band) is the hallmark of ESIPT-active members of this family.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study has a phenolic OH at the 2-position of the phenoxy ring relative to the hydrazone nitrogen linkage. Whether a six-membered or five-membered H-bond ring can form between this OH and the hydrazone N is the central unresolved ESIPT precondition. This card provides the geometric criteria (O···N distance, angle, ring size) needed to evaluate that precondition.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT in salicylaldehyde hydrazones requires ortho-OH relative to imine N, forming a six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring; para or meta substitution precludes ESIPT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: X-ray crystallography and DFT geometry optimization of salicylaldehyde hydrazone series; UV-vis and fluorescence in solution and solid state.

evidence_cards.source: Dyes and Pigments / Journal of Luminescence – phenol-imine Schiff base ICT vs ESIPT discrimination
evidence_cards.title: ICT versus ESIPT in phenol-imine Schiff bases: role of donor-acceptor geometry and H-bond ring size
evidence_cards.observation: In phenol-imine Schiff bases bearing extended aromatic acceptors (e.g., anthracene, naphthalene, acridine), the dominant emission mechanism depends on whether the phenolic OH is positioned to form an intramolecular H-bond with the imine nitrogen. When the OH is ortho to the C=N bond (salicylaldimine motif), ESIPT is observed with a large Stokes shift (>100 nm) and a tautomer emission band. When the OH is remote from the imine N (e.g., connected via an alkoxy tether or at a non-ortho position), ICT dominates: emission is solvatochromic, Stokes shift is moderate (40–80 nm), and no tautomer band appears. The presence of a strong electron-withdrawing acceptor aryl group (such as anthracen-9-yl) enhances ICT character but does not by itself induce ESIPT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule contains an acridanthracene-type (9,10-diarylanthracene / anthracen-9-yl) acceptor linked via a hydrazone to a phenol bearing an alkoxy tether. This card addresses whether the remote positioning of the phenolic OH (connected through an ethyleneoxy chain rather than directly ortho to the imine) would preclude ESIPT and favor ICT, and what spectral signatures distinguish the two.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Remote or tether-connected phenolic OH relative to imine N precludes ESIPT; ICT dominates with solvatochromic, moderate-Stokes-shift emission.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Fluorescence spectroscopy in solvents of varying polarity; comparison of ortho-OH vs. non-ortho-OH Schiff base series.

evidence_cards.source: Chemical Society Reviews – ICT vs TICT mechanistic discrimination in donor-acceptor fluorophores
evidence_cards.title: Distinguishing ICT from TICT: photophysical criteria and structural determinants in donor-acceptor dyes
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews of ICT vs TICT mechanisms identify the following discriminating observables: (1) TICT is associated with a viscosity-dependent emission intensity (restricted rotation in viscous media or solid state restores emission), a red-shifted structureless emission band in polar solvents, and often dual emission (locally excited + TICT band). (2) ICT shows solvatochromic red-shift of a single emission band with increasing solvent polarity but does not require a twisted geometry; emission is not strongly viscosity-dependent unless rotation is also involved. (3) Structural determinants for TICT include a freely rotatable bond between donor and acceptor moieties with a low rotational barrier; planar, rigid, or sterically hindered donor-acceptor junctions disfavor TICT. (4) In hydrazone-based AIEgens with restricted rotation in aggregates, the AIE effect is mechanistically consistent with RIR (restriction of intramolecular rotation) suppressing a non-radiative TICT or dark-state pathway, but the ground-state emission mechanism in solution is often ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the photophysical criteria needed to distinguish ICT from TICT for the molecule under study. The molecule has a hydrazone rotor (C=N–N linkage) and an anthracene acceptor; the card clarifies what experimental observables (viscosity dependence, dual emission, solvatochromism pattern) would discriminate the two hypotheses.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires freely rotatable donor-acceptor bond and shows viscosity-dependent, red-shifted, often dual emission; ICT shows solvatochromic single-band emission without strong viscosity dependence.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; time-resolved fluorescence; comparison of rigid vs. flexible donor-acceptor analogs.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2014, 43, 4563–4601 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Phys. Chem. A 2019, 123, 2867–2877 (salicylaldehyde-hydrazone ESIPT study)
evidence_cards.title: ESIPT in salicylaldehyde-hydrazone Schiff bases: geometric requirements and six-membered ring H-bond motif
evidence_cards.observation: Salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazones and Schiff bases bearing a phenolic OH ortho to the imine/hydrazone C=N linkage are well-documented ESIPT scaffolds. The ESIPT process requires a six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring (O-H···N=C) with O···N distance typically <2.7 Å and near-linear O-H···N angle. In these systems, the phenolic OH acts as proton donor and the imine/hydrazone N as acceptor. ESIPT is reported as a dual-emission process (normal vs tautomer band). When the phenolic OH is not ortho to the C=N nitrogen (i.e., the H-bond ring would be five- or seven-membered, or the geometry is non-planar), ESIPT is suppressed and ICT or fluorescence quenching dominates instead.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a phenolic OH and a hydrazone-like C=N/N=N linkage. Whether the phenolic OH is geometrically preorganized in a six-membered ring toward the imine/hydrazone N is the key ESIPT discriminator. This card establishes the geometric threshold and ring-size requirement that must be met for ESIPT to be operative in this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT in phenol-imine/hydrazone systems requires six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring with O···N < 2.7 Å; non-ortho or non-planar arrangements suppress ESIPT in favor of ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Crystallographic and solution-phase photophysical studies of salicylaldehyde hydrazone derivatives; dual emission as ESIPT diagnostic.

evidence_cards.source: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2016, 138, 14343–14354; Dyes Pigm. 2020, 174, 108042 (acylhydrazone ESIPT/ICT competition)
evidence_cards.title: Acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone fluorophores: ESIPT suppression by remote donor-acceptor substitution and ether linkage
evidence_cards.observation: In acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone fluorophores, introduction of electron-donating groups (e.g., alkoxy, amino) at positions remote from the phenolic OH-to-imine H-bond site is reported to modulate the ICT character without necessarily enabling or disabling ESIPT. Specifically, when the phenolic OH is para or meta to the hydrazone linkage rather than ortho, ESIPT is not observed and the emission is assigned to ICT. Ether-linked substituents (e.g., -OCH2CH2-) on the aromatic ring bearing the phenolic OH are noted to increase electron density and red-shift ICT emission but do not create new ESIPT pathways. The presence of an azide or diazo group (N3, N=N=N) as a remote substituent does not alter the ESIPT geometry requirement.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule bears an azide/diazo group and an oxyethylene ether tether on the phenolic ring. This card addresses whether such remote substitution patterns (ether linkage, electron-donating groups) are known to preserve or suppress ESIPT relative to ICT in phenol-hydrazone scaffolds, directly relevant to the main gap.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Remote ether or alkoxy substitution on phenol-hydrazone scaffolds modulates ICT but does not create ESIPT pathways; ESIPT requires ortho OH-to-imine geometry regardless of remote substitution.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution fluorescence, solvatochromism, and pH-dependent emission studies of acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone dyes.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Commun. 2018, 54, 7136–7155 (review: AIE-active Schiff bases and hydrazones); J. Mater. Chem. C 2021, 9, 11517
evidence_cards.title: AIE-active phenol-imine and hydrazone Schiff bases: ICT vs ESIPT assignment criteria and acridine/anthracene scaffold context
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews of AIE-active Schiff bases and hydrazones bearing phenolic OH groups report that ESIPT is operative when the OH is ortho to the C=N nitrogen, forming a planar six-membered ring. When the phenolic OH is at a position that would require a five-membered or non-planar H-bond ring, or when the imine N is part of a hydrazone (C=N-N) rather than a simple imine (C=N), ESIPT efficiency is reduced or absent. For acridine- and anthracene-based hydrazone AIEgens, the dominant photophysical mechanism reported is ICT (with RIR contributing to AIE), not ESIPT, even when a phenolic OH is present elsewhere in the molecule. The acridine/anthracene core acts as the electron acceptor in ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains an acridine-like (9,10-dihydroacridine/anthracene-fused) core as the electron acceptor and a hydrazone linkage. This card addresses whether ESIPT or ICT is the dominant mechanism for this specific scaffold class and what geometric conditions distinguish them.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: For anthracene/acridine-based hydrazone AIEgens with phenolic OH, ICT (not ESIPT) is the dominant emission mechanism; ESIPT requires ortho OH-to-imine geometry not typically present in these scaffolds.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solid-state and solution fluorescence, AIE titration, and DFT studies of Schiff base and hydrazone AIEgens.

evidence_cards.source: Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2017, 19, 28242–28250; J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2020, 11, 7890–7897 (ESIPT geometry screening in phenol-imine fluorophores)
evidence_cards.title: Geometric screening of ESIPT plausibility in phenol-imine fluorophores: O···N distance and ring-size as decisive criteria
evidence_cards.observation: Computational and crystallographic screening of phenol-imine fluorophores reports that ESIPT is geometrically feasible only when the O···N distance is below ~2.65 Å and the H-bond ring is six-membered (five-atom chain: O-H···N=C-C). When the phenolic OH is separated from the imine N by more than one bond (e.g., through a vinyl or azo bridge), the H-bond ring becomes seven-membered or larger, and ESIPT is not observed experimentally. In hydrazone systems (C=N-NH), the hydrazone NH rather than phenolic OH is often the ESIPT donor. For molecules where the phenolic OH and the hydrazone N are not in a direct ortho relationship, the photophysics is dominated by ICT, with emission solvatochromism as the diagnostic. The presence of a C=N/N=N azo bridge between the phenolic ring and the hydrazone acceptor extends the H-bond ring beyond six members and is associated with ESIPT suppression.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a C=N/N=N linkage between the phenolic ring and the acridine acceptor. This card directly addresses whether such a bridge geometry is associated with ESIPT feasibility or suppression, and provides the O···N distance and ring-size criteria that the microscopic geometry descriptors (available in the workflow) should be evaluated against.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT is suppressed when the phenolic OH-to-imine N H-bond ring exceeds six members or O···N > 2.65 Å; azo/vinyl bridges between phenol and hydrazone extend the ring and are associated with ESIPT suppression and ICT dominance.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: DFT geometry optimization, X-ray crystallography, and solution fluorescence of phenol-imine and hydrazone fluorophores with systematic variation of bridge length.

evidence_cards.title: Geometric screening of ESIPT plausibility in phenol-imine fluorophores: O···N distance and ring-size as decisive criteria
evidence_cards.observation: Computational and crystallographic screening of phenol-imine fluorophores reports that ESIPT is geometrically feasible only when the O···N distance is below ~2.65 Å and the H-bond ring is six-membered (five-atom chain: O-H···N=C-C). When the phenolic OH is separated from the imine N by more than one bond (e.g., through a vinyl or azo bridge), the H-bond ring becomes seven-membered or larger, and ESIPT is not observed experimentally. In hydrazone systems (C=N-NH), the hydrazone NH rather than phenolic OH is often the ESIPT donor. For molecules where the phenolic OH and the hydrazone N are not in a direct ortho relationship, the photophysics is dominated by ICT, with emission solvatochromism as the diagnostic. The presence of a C=N/N=N azo bridge between the phenolic ring and the hydrazone acceptor extends the H-bond ring beyond six members and is associated with ESIPT suppression.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a C=N/N=N linkage between the phenolic ring and the acridine acceptor. This card directly addresses whether such a bridge geometry is associated with ESIPT feasibility or suppression, and provides the O···N distance and ring-size criteria that the microscopic geometry descriptors (available in the workflow) should be evaluated against.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT is suppressed when the phenolic OH-to-imine N H-bond ring exceeds six members or O···N > 2.65 Å; azo/vinyl bridges between phenol and hydrazone extend the ring and are associated with ESIPT suppression and ICT dominance.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: DFT geometry optimization, X-ray crystallography, and solution fluorescence of phenol-imine and hydrazone fluorophores with systematic variation of bridge length.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2014, 43, 4563–4601 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Phys. Chem. A 2019, 123, 2867–2877 (salicylaldehyde-hydrazone ESIPT study)
evidence_cards.title: ESIPT in salicylaldehyde-hydrazone Schiff bases: geometric requirements and six-membered ring H-bond motif
evidence_cards.observation: Salicylaldehyde-derived hydrazones and Schiff bases bearing a phenolic OH ortho to the imine/hydrazone C=N linkage are well-documented ESIPT scaffolds. The ESIPT process requires a six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring (O-H···N=C) with O···N distance typically <2.7 Å and near-linear O-H···N angle. In these systems, the phenolic OH acts as proton donor and the imine/hydrazone N as acceptor. ESIPT is reported as a dual-emission process (normal vs tautomer band). When the phenolic OH is not ortho to the C=N nitrogen (i.e., the H-bond ring would be five- or seven-membered, or the geometry is non-planar), ESIPT is suppressed and ICT or fluorescence quenching dominates instead.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a phenolic OH and a hydrazone-like C=N/N=N linkage. Whether the phenolic OH is geometrically preorganized in a six-membered ring toward the imine/hydrazone N is the key ESIPT discriminator. This card establishes the geometric threshold and ring-size requirement that must be met for ESIPT to be operative in this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ESIPT in phenol-imine/hydrazone systems requires six-membered intramolecular H-bond ring with O···N < 2.7 Å; non-ortho or non-planar arrangements suppress ESIPT in favor of ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Crystallographic and solution-phase photophysical studies of salicylaldehyde hydrazone derivatives; dual emission as ESIPT diagnostic.

evidence_cards.source: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2016, 138, 14343–14354; Dyes Pigm. 2020, 174, 108042 (acylhydrazone ESIPT/ICT competition)
evidence_cards.title: Acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone fluorophores: ESIPT suppression by remote donor-acceptor substitution and ether linkage
evidence_cards.observation: In acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone fluorophores, introduction of electron-donating groups (e.g., alkoxy, amino) at positions remote from the phenolic OH-to-imine H-bond site is reported to modulate the ICT character without necessarily enabling or disabling ESIPT. Specifically, when the phenolic OH is para or meta to the hydrazone linkage rather than ortho, ESIPT is not observed and the emission is assigned to ICT. Ether-linked substituents (e.g., -OCH2CH2-) on the aromatic ring bearing the phenolic OH are noted to increase electron density and red-shift ICT emission but do not create new ESIPT pathways. The presence of an azide or diazo group (N3, N=N=N) as a remote substituent does not alter the ESIPT geometry requirement.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule bears an azide/diazo group and an oxyethylene ether tether on the phenolic ring. This card addresses whether such remote substitution patterns (ether linkage, electron-donating groups) are known to preserve or suppress ESIPT relative to ICT in phenol-hydrazone scaffolds, directly relevant to the main gap.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Remote ether or alkoxy substitution on phenol-hydrazone scaffolds modulates ICT but does not create ESIPT pathways; ESIPT requires ortho OH-to-imine geometry regardless of remote substitution.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution fluorescence, solvatochromism, and pH-dependent emission studies of acylhydrazone and phenol-hydrazone dyes.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Commun. 2018, 54, 7136–7155 (review: AIE-active Schiff bases and hydrazones); J. Mater. Chem. C 2021, 9, 11517
evidence_cards.title: AIE-active phenol-imine and hydrazone Schiff bases: ICT vs ESIPT assignment criteria and acridine/anthracene scaffold context
evidence_cards.observation: Reviews of AIE-active Schiff bases and hydrazones bearing phenolic OH groups report that ESIPT is operative when the OH is ortho to the C=N nitrogen, forming a planar six-membered ring. When the phenolic OH is at a position that would require a five-membered or non-planar H-bond ring, or when the imine N is part of a hydrazone (C=N-N) rather than a simple imine (C=N), ESIPT efficiency is reduced or absent. For acridine- and anthracene-based hydrazone AIEgens, the dominant photophysical mechanism reported is ICT (with RIR contributing to AIE), not ESIPT, even when a phenolic OH is present elsewhere in the molecule. The acridine/anthracene core acts as the electron acceptor in ICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains an acridine-like (9,10-dihydroacridine/anthracene-fused) core as the electron acceptor and a hydrazone linkage. This card addresses whether ESIPT or ICT is the dominant mechanism for this specific scaffold class and what geometric conditions distinguish them.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: For anthracene/acridine-based hydrazone AIEgens with phenolic OH, ICT (not ESIPT) is the dominant emission mechanism; ESIPT requires ortho OH-to-imine geometry not typically present in these scaffolds.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solid-state and solution fluorescence, AIE titration, and DFT studies of Schiff base and hydrazone AIEgens.

Report whether the external evidence directly supports ESIPT, directly challenges ESIPT, or leaves it unresolved for this scaffold class. Do not decide the global mechanism.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Macro screening supports a strong donor-acceptor layout with extended conjugation, and the baseline vertical excitation is reasonably bright and compatible with charge-transfer-like excitation in a largely planar scaffold. This strengthens ICT modestly but not decisively because no direct pairwise discriminator versus TICT or ESIPT has yet been run.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT remains credible because the molecule still has multiple rotatable bonds and donor-acceptor partitioning, but the very high planarity proxy and extended conjugation weaken a strong prior for twisted-state dominance at baseline geometry.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ESIPT remains chemically plausible due to the phenolic OH and nearby imine/hydrazone-like nitrogens, but the current evidence has not directly established a favorable intramolecular proton-transfer geometry or tautomeric preorganization.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The large fused aromatic core could support locally excited behavior, but donor-acceptor partitioning and heteroatom-rich substitution keep a purely neutral aromatic explanation secondary.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Known mechanistic motifs still explain the current evidence better than an unspecified mechanism, so unknown stays as a low-probability reserve.

hypothesis_screening_ledger.screening_note: Baseline macro and microscopic evidence indirectly weaken TICT: strong donor-acceptor character is present, but a very high planarity proxy and extended conjugation make twisted-state dominance less favored at initialization. TICT is still unresolved because no direct torsion-sensitivity screening has yet been performed.

ESIPT remains in direct coverage debt. The structure contains a phenolic OH and nearby imine/hydrazone-like nitrogens, but no direct geometric precondition screen for an intramolecular H-bond or proton-transfer-ready contact has yet been run.
The aromatic core is substantial, but donor-acceptor partitioning and bright low-energy excitation make a purely neutral aromatic explanation less competitive.
Unknown is retained only as a low residual reserve because named mechanisms explain the current evidence better.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The requested observables relate to detailed molecular geometry and excited-state characterization relevant to ESIPT. The baseline bundle action (run_baseline_bundle) provides low-cost S0 optimization and vertical excited-state manifold characterization, which can produce molecule-specific geometry and excited-state data. This action is the only one in the registry that directly supports the requested deliverables and observables with new calculations, as prior artifact parsing has already been done and found insufficient.

Other bounded follow-up actions (conformer_bundle, torsion_snapshots) focus on conformer or torsion sensitivity and do not explicitly target the requested ESIPT-specific geometry and orientation observables. Targeted fixed-geometry analyses operate on existing artifact bundles and do not generate new geometry or excited-state data, so they cannot fulfill the requirement to change the observable from prior parse-only attempts.

Therefore, the best supported single-action translation is to run the baseline bundle with new calculation and ground-state optimization enabled, which will produce the low-cost S0 geometry optimization and vertical excited-state manifold characterization needed to assess the ESIPT-relevant molecular geometry and excited states.

No explicit hard-binding to a different capability is present, so this substitution is exact and fulfills the local evidence goal exactly.
Capability limit note: The current Amesp registry does not expose a specialized ESIPT-specific geometry or proton-transfer coordinate scan or analysis. The baseline bundle is the minimal supported route that can produce new molecule-specific geometry and excited-state data relevant to ESIPT. More specialized ESIPT observables (e.g., explicit O-H···N orientation or six-membered ring proton transfer compatibility) must be inferred from the baseline bundle outputs or require future capability extensions.

No capability blockade is indicated; the baseline bundle is fully supported and appropriate for this task.
Failure policy: If the baseline bundle calculation fails or does not produce the requested observables, the failure should be reported explicitly. No fallback or proxy action is appropriate because the baseline bundle is the minimal exact supported action for this task.

If the prepared structure is unavailable or incompatible, the action cannot proceed and should report unsupported.

No partial fulfillment or proxy fallback is applicable.

raw_response.diagnosis: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for the given molecule from an initial portfolio of ICT, TICT, ESIPT, neutral aromatic, and unknown. The current working top1 is ICT and the current runner-up is TICT. ESIPT remains an additional credible portfolio member because the structure contains a phenolic OH and a Schiff-base-like N-containing motif that could support proton-transfer-related photophysics. ICT is only a working hypothesis because the present evidence is structural and proxy-level: the molecule has donor-acceptor character, extended conjugation, and a high planarity proxy, but also several rotatable bonds that could support twisting and nonradiative charge-transfer relaxation. There is visible uncertainty between ICT and TICT, and ESIPT is not yet screened out. Shared prepared structure context is available and ready, including a selected conformer and prepared xyz/sdf paths, so both specialized agents can reuse the same starting structure. Macro can inspect motif-level donor/acceptor partitioning, conjugation, likely proton-transfer preconditions, and whether the architecture more naturally supports planar ICT versus twist-enabled relaxation, but Macro cannot make definitive electronic-state assignments. Microscopic can perform one low-cost baseline-only S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure to provide indirect evidence on geometry preconditions and state-ordering/brightness features, but cannot in this first round resolve conformer sensitivity, torsional dependence, or multi-step excited-state pathways. Because both TICT and ESIPT remain unscreened credible alternatives, the first round should gather both macro and microscopic evidence and remain in portfolio screening rather than collapse prematurely into pairwise contraction.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run a first-round portfolio-screening pass with both Macro and Microscopic. Macro should screen the open alternatives TICT and ESIPT against the current ICT lead using only structure-level reasoning. Microscopic should perform exactly one bounded baseline S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure to provide indirect evidence relevant to planar ICT versus twist-enabled or proton-transfer-enabled alternatives.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Using the provided shared structure and molecule identity only, produce a portfolio-neutral screening report. Focus on whether the architecture supports ICT, TICT, and ESIPT as plausible AIE-related mechanisms. Comment on donor/acceptor partitioning, conjugation continuity, likely rigidity versus twist freedom, and whether a phenolic OH and nearby N-containing motif appear geometrically capable of intramolecular H-bonding or proton-transfer preconditioning. Do not make a final global mechanism decision; instead report which of TICT and ESIPT remain credible after structure-level screening and what observations would most reduce uncertainty next.
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Perform exactly one low-cost baseline-only S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure under the supported first-round scope: reuse the shared structure, run the baseline S0 optimization and bounded S1 vertical excitation only. Report only local results relevant to portfolio screening, such as whether the baseline geometry remains broadly planar/extended, whether the lowest excited-state picture looks more consistent with delocalized charge-transfer versus ordinary local excitation at this baseline level, and any obvious artifact warnings. Do not run conformer sensitivity, torsion scans, follow-up steps, or make final mechanism judgments.

decision.diagnosis: Top1 is ICT at 0.63 and top2 is TICT at 0.14, with ESIPT reduced to 0.08 after verifier reweighting. I interpret the verifier evidence as mixed but net anti-ESIPT for this case: it supports that ESIPT is plausible in phenol-imine/hydrazone families only when a favorable six-membered O-H···N arrangement is present, and it also supplies close-family context for ICT competition and ESIPT suppression under some substitution patterns. That helps reduce portfolio coverage debt qualitatively by narrowing what would count as positive ESIPT support, but it does not fully discharge the debt because the remaining question is molecule-specific and still lacks direct scaffold-level contact/orientation evidence. A still-credible hypothesis, ESIPT, remains only indirectly weakened rather than directly screened or blocked by capability, so the case legitimately stays in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction. Closure justification is still missing, not sufficient. The case is not ready for decisive finalize or best-available finalize; it needs one bounded follow-up focused on directly screening or formally blocking ESIPT.
